excerpt: The marks themselves—aʿrāb, ⟪ar:اعراب⟫, ⟪ar:تشدید⟫ (intensification), and ⟪ar:جزم⟫ (cessation)—constitute far more than ornamental signs: they transform meaning itself.
source_authority: High confidence from Fatimid grammatical-esoteric tradition
source_location_hint: Discussion of diacritical meanings in relation to exegesis

excerpt: Surah al-Ḥamd (Surah 1) contains 21 letters of the first three types and omits entirely the seven letters of the oppressive heaviness.
source_authority: High confidence from Ismaili exegetical tradition

excerpt: The Qur'an's miracle manifests in the fact that its memorizers number in the thousands across the world.
source_authority: High confidence from classical Ismaili juristic commentary
source_location_hint: On the preservation and memorization of the Qur'an

excerpt: In terms of numerical reckoning (ḥisāb al-jummal), alif's value is one, signifying that the First Intellect is the cause of all existence and the wellspring of their becoming.
source_authority: High confidence from Ismaili cosmological tradition
source_location_hint: On numerical values and the First Intellect

excerpt: In the interpretation of the Qur'an, the diacritical letters represent believers who have recently entered the summons of faith through covenant with the Prophet. As they become obedient to their Lord, blessings kindle within them and their forms begin to grow noble.
source_authority: High confidence from Ismaili doctrinal exposition
source_location_hint: On believers as letters and their spiritual development

excerpt: Intensification (tashdīd) is that in which the middle letter is pronounced twice, producing speech's intensity, as exemplified in the verse commanding conveyance of revelation.
source_authority: High confidence from Ismaili grammatical commentary
source_location_hint: On grammatical intensification in Qur'anic usage

excerpt: In a span of one cycle (360 kars), a believer's earthly and occult natures progress to the heart's place, and the heart ascends to the Natīqa's place.
source_authority: High confidence from Ismaili cosmological cycles
source_location_hint: On believer's spiritual progression through cyclical time

excerpt: The nine diacritical surahs refer to nine periods spanning 9,000 years; the celestial forces that aided the Prophet at Badr manifest again in the cosmic ages.
The divine command (Amr Allah) refers to the Amīr al-Muʾminīn; do not hasten his manifestation, for it is partial in this age and complete only with the Qāʾim.
The verse applies partially to ʿAlī and wholly to the Qāʾim, whose station encompasses the highest spiritual realities pertaining to the Hereafter.

The twenty-nine disjoined letters signify Muḥammad as the twenty-ninth Imām, completing a full cycle; the twenty-ninth number corresponds to the date of the Kaʿbah's revelation, the 29th of Dhū al-Qaʿdah.
The forty-nine disjoined letters of the second half refer to the forty-nine Imāms distributed across seven weeks; the fiftieth represents the comprehensive establishment in the age of unveiling.
The letters ʿAyn-Mīm (ʿAlī and Muḥammad) transcend the disjoined-letter category, standing as the culmination and meaning-bestower of all prior letters, inseparable and eternal.
The reversal of ʿAyn-Mīm into Mīm-ʿAyn (Maʿ) signifies togetherness; Muḥammad and ʿAlī are never separated, perpetually manifesting together across all epochs.
source_location_hint: eternal pairing of Muḥammad and ʿAlī

excerpt: The twenty-two daily prayer cycles indicate that after twenty Imāms of concealment, the Proof would appear; the Prophet established this number as a sign of the final two Imāms' emergence.
In the sacred temple (Haikāl) of the Qāʾim: ʿAlī holds the position of the heart, Muḥammad the intellect, the Imāms are the eyes, Fāṭimah the neck, and Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq the tongue.
The five instances of Alif-Lām-Mīm correspond to the Five Purified Ones (Panjtān Pāk) and their parallels among the five prophetic witnesses (Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus).
Khā-Ḥā (Khā-Ḥā) denotes the Messenger, who gathered the five letters of the Qur'an and assembled the Five Sacred Traces (Khamsat al-Āthār).
Al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn stand in parity; all Imāms after them are Ḥasanī or Ḥusaynī. In al-Ḥusayn's temple, al-Ḥasan occupies the heart, for the heart itself is woven from al-Ḥasan's temple.
ʿAlī Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn unified all four stations (Prophethood, Guardianship, Imamate, Will) into one; he is called ʿĀdam al-Awliyāʾ (the Adam of the Friends of God).
Four supreme Imāms of the seven-thousand-year epoch—Muḥammad ibn Ismāʿīl, ʿAlī al-Ṭayyib, Aḥmad ibn Muḥammad, and Abū al-Qāʾim—embody the entire age; of these, Abū al-Qāʾim stands supreme.
ʿAlī shall advance toward his eternal form as ʿĀdam's counterpart; Muḥammad shall be his caliph; together they remain inseparable—Mīm and ʿAyn never parted.
Yā-Sīn signifies the heart of the era; Surah Yā-Sīn is the 23rd, as is Laylat al-Qadr in Ramaḍān, and as is Imām al-Ṭayyib when the first thirty Imāms are divided into seven parts.
